macro_events: DEAL PROGRESSING — On Mon 15-Jun, President Trump and VP Vance virtually signed the US-Iran framework agreement at the G7 summit; the formal MOU signing is set for 19-Jun in Switzerland (Pakistan-mediated), extending the ceasefire 60 days. Critically, the Strait of Hormuz is to open for mine-clearance only AFTER the 19-Jun signing — it is not yet physically reopened, and officials caution 'challenges remain.' This unwinds the dominant aluminum war premium but on a delayed, conditional timeline.

Physical reality check: the deal restores the Gulf shipping/logistics path but not the damaged capacity. EGA's Al Taweelah still needs ~12 mo to restore regardless of the truce; ALBA remains impaired; a 3–6 mo lag separates a geopolitical resolution from actual metal supply — the structural deficit persists.

Market reaction: aluminum sold off to a one-month low on Mon 15-Jun as the war premium deflated, then REBOUNDED to ~$3,550 (16-Jun) as risk sentiment improved and the structural deficit reasserted — a regime shift, not a collapse. Oil fell hard (Brent below $86.5, lowest since early March; WTI sub-$85) and global equities rallied (Nikkei +2%).

Hot May US inflation (CPI +0.5% m/m, +4.2% y/y released 10-Jun; PPI +1.1% m/m) keeps the Fed hawkish (~70% odds of a Dec hike) and the USD firm (~99.9) — a headwind for $-metals; the deal's oil-price relief is disinflationary at the margin and may temper that hawkishness over time.
China unwrought aluminum + semis exports +15.5% YoY to 632,000 t in May (5-mo cumulative ~2.69 Mt, +10.4% YoY) — adds ex-China supply/competition.
lme_commentary: Commentary: The confirmed official series runs through 12-Jun ($3,536 cash); the 15-Jun official print is still pending. Monday's post-deal session drove the benchmark to a one-month low as the Gulf war premium deflated, but futures have since rebounded to ~$3,550 (16-Jun) — the structural deficit is providing a floor even as the geopolitical premium unwinds (inventories ~320kt, down ~37% YTD from ~509kt in Jan, ~1.4 days of global output, ~92% Russian-origin; cash-3M backwardation already compressed from 19-yr highs (~$69 on 08-Jun) to ~$4). Net: a lower, more volatile but supported price. The 19-Jun formal signing and the actual Strait reopening (mine-clearance is post-signing) are the next catalysts; the unrepaired Gulf supply gap (EGA ~12 mo; ALBA impaired) caps the downside.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: Latest confirmed index; Q2 oversupply pressuring

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$366/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer Platts API region (~$305/t); surplus building on higher production/shipments into subdued demand.

Hormuz reopening (after the 19-Jun signing, via mine-clearance) reverses the rerouting premium — alumina cargo flows through the Gulf should normalize over coming weeks, easing the logistics distortion that had benefited China.
Bauxite: Guinea's stricter export controls still tighten raw-material supply and add upstream cost pressure — a structural offset independent of the Gulf truce.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply disruption that lifted regional refining costs should ease as the blockade lifts; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery may restart ahead of the smelter.
Net: input-cost picture skews softer post-deal (alumina ample, energy/logistics relief), though Guinea bauxite controls keep a floor under raw-material risk.
net_read: REGIME SHIFT, NOT A COLLAPSE — WAR PREMIUM DEFLATING, PHYSICAL DEFICIT INTACT.  Aluminum's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock and Hormuz blockade; the US-Iran deal — virtually signed by Trump and Vance at the G7 on 15-Jun, with formal signing set for 19-Jun (Switzerland) under a 60-day ceasefire — unwinds the acute geopolitical risk premium. The market's own verdict is instructive: prices dropped to a one-month low on Monday's deflation, then rebounded to ~$3,550 (16-Jun) as the structural floor held. That floor is real: EGA's Al Taweelah still needs ~12 mo to restore, ALBA remains impaired, the Strait physically reopens only AFTER the 19-Jun signing (mine-clearance), LME stocks keep drawing (~320kt, ~1.4 days of output, ~92% Russian-origin), and a 3–6 mo lag separates the diplomatic resolution from actual metal. Add a firm USD (hot May CPI +4.2% y/y; ~70% odds of a Dec Fed hike) and rising China exports (+15.5% YoY in May). Expect a lower, more volatile but supported price. Watch: the 15-Jun official LME print, the 19-Jun signing / ceasefire implementation ('challenges remain'), the actual Strait reopening, EGA/ALBA restart timing, and the USD/Fed path.

feed.text: Aluminum rebounds to ~$3,550 from Monday's one-month low as the structural deficit reasserts and risk sentiment improves — the initial war-premium selloff finds support. LME 15-Jun official print still pending.

Trump and VP Vance virtually sign the US-Iran framework agreement at the G7 summit; formal MOU signing set for 19-Jun in Switzerland (Pakistan-mediated). Strait of Hormuz to open for mine-clearance only AFTER signing; 60-day ceasefire — officials warn 'challenges remain.'
Aluminum drops to a one-month low as the Gulf war premium deflates on the deal; oil slides (Brent below $86.5, lowest since early March; WTI sub-$85) and global equities rally (Nikkei +2%).

Physical reality check: the deal restores the Gulf shipping path but not damaged capacity — EGA's Al Taweelah still ~12 mo to restore, ALBA impaired; a 3–6 mo lag separates resolution from metal supply. Structural deficit persists.
US and Iran reach a framework deal to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz; US naval blockade to be removed. Removes the dominant aluminum war premium that drove the 4-yr-high rally.

Pre-deal, LME official cash settled $3,536 (+1.1% d/d), 3M $3,532 — last confirmed official print; 15-Jun official still pending. Aluminum had steadied after an ~8% pullback from the 02-Jun 4-yr high.
LME inventory drew to 319,925 t (−2,075 d/d), down ~37% YTD; ~92% Russian-origin keeps available units scarce — the deficit is unaffected by the deal.
Hot May US CPI (+0.5% m/m, +4.2% y/y) reinforces ~70% odds of a Dec Fed hike and a firm USD — a near-term cap on $-metals.

news.headline: US-Iran framework deal virtually signed 15-Jun at the G7; formal MOU signing 19-Jun (Switzerland), Hormuz to open for mine-clearance after signing, blockade removed; 60-day ceasefire — 'challenges remain.' Removes the dominant aluminum war premium.
news.source: NPR / PBS / Al Jazeera / CNN / NBC (14–15 Jun)

news.headline: Aluminum hit a one-month low Mon 15-Jun on the premium deflation, then rebounded to ~$3,550 (16-Jun) as structural tightness reasserted — a regime shift, not a collapse.
Deal restores Gulf logistics but not capacity: ~9% of global / ~25% of non-China supply still impaired. EGA ~12 mo to restore Al Taweelah; ALBA still down — structural deficit intact.
Cash-3M backwardation compressed from 19-yr highs (~$69) to ~$4 — acute front-end scarcity easing, though total stocks stay low.
LME stocks draw to ~320kt (−37% YTD), ~1.4 days of global output; ~92% Russian-origin. Deficit unaffected by the deal.

Oil falls sharply post-deal (Brent below $86.5, lowest since early March; WTI sub-$85) as Hormuz reopening deflates the risk premium; disinflationary at the margin, though hot May CPI keeps the Fed hawkish and USD firm.
Unwrought Al + semis exports +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May; 5-mo cumulative ~2.69 Mt (+10.4%).
Q2-26 oversupply pressuring FOB Australia alumina (~$366/t); Hormuz reopening (post-signing) normalizes rerouted cargoes.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 16-Jun (no new releases since the prior run — Q2 season begins mid-July: Alcoa ~mid-Jul, Norsk Hydro late-Jul). Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com, Zawya/TradeArabia, GuruFocus.

commercial.text: Monday's one-month-low-then-rebound to ~$3,550 validates the stance: the deal removed the war premium but the unrepaired Gulf capacity (EGA ~12 mo) and low LME stocks cushion the downside. Accelerate near-dated realization and ensure LME hedges are in place on priced tonnes, but do NOT chase the top with new forward fixed-price commitments AND do NOT over-hedge forward at distressed levels — the rebound shows two-way risk. Maximise value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture while it lasts.

The Gulf outage drove MJP Q2 +79.5% q/q (~$350–353) and record US MW premiums; the Hormuz reopening (post the 19-Jun signing) will, over time, normalize the premium support. Lock in elevated premiums on renewals and spot allocations NOW, before the physical-tightness lag (3–6 mo) gives way to normalized flows. As a non-disrupted supplier, capture premium share while EGA/ALBA remain impaired.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$366/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer API region (~$305/t); the truce reverses the Hormuz rerouting premium. If net seller, lock firm ex-China pricing on remaining strength; if buyer for smelting, exploit softer spot windows as Gulf flows normalize.

Hormuz reopening and blockade removal should lower Gulf freight, war-risk insurance and routing risk — but the Strait physically reopens only after the 19-Jun signing (mine-clearance), so the relief is not yet live. Sequence the stand-down carefully: keep contingency routing and demurrage clauses live through the 60-day ceasefire until normalization is confirmed.

Manage the transition narrative with strategic accounts: physical tightness persists through the lag even as the geopolitical risk recedes. Protect OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and convert reliability through the volatility into relationship equity and premium tolerance.

Track: (1) the 15-Jun official LME print and the scale of the premium unwind vs the rebound; (2) deal implementation — 19-Jun signing, the actual Strait reopening, 60-day ceasefire durability ('challenges remain'); (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines; (4) USD/Fed path (hot May CPI, Dec decision); (5) China export pace & Section 232 tariffs; (6) Guinea bauxite / alumina balance.

bottom_line: A regime shift, not a collapse — and Monday's one-month-low-then-rebound to ~$3,550 proves it. The 15-Jun virtual signing of the US-Iran deal (formal signing 19-Jun, 60-day ceasefire) unwinds the geopolitical war premium and points to a lower, choppier price — but the unrepaired Gulf capacity, drawing LME stocks and 3–6 month supply lag keep a structural floor, and the Strait physically reopens only after the 19-Jun signing. For a reliable Gulf-adjacent producer: lock in elevated premiums and near-dated realization before flows normalize, hedge priced tonnes through the volatility, and avoid over-committing forward at distressed levels — the rebound shows the downside is cushioned.

caveats: The US-Iran deal was virtually signed 15-Jun but the formal MOU signing is set for 19-Jun under a 60-day ceasefire; officials warn 'challenges remain.' The Strait of Hormuz reopens for commercial shipping only after the signing (via mine-clearance) — logistics relief is announced but not yet physically live, and damaged Gulf smelting capacity is unrepaired.

LME official cash/3M settlements publish with a short lag; the latest confirmed official close is 12-Jun (Westmetall) and the 15-Jun official print is still pending. The ~$3,550 level is the latest live benchmark futures read (post one-month-low rebound), not an official settlement.

Macro/FX/energy values are the latest confirmed reads and are point-in-time; oil (Brent/WTI) and gold are reconciled to the confirmed post-deal direction (oil down sharply, safe-haven easing) but specific levels should be re-validated against live feeds before use.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus). Values shown are most recent public anchors / status — reconcile to the desk's licensed feed before pricing.

This report is market intelligence, not trading or investment advice. Figures are point-in-time and should be re-validated before commercial commitments.

outlook.curve_note: Front of the curve is backwardated (cash ≈ 3M) — a tight-now signal. Consensus sees a firm 2026 average then an easing into 2027 as Gulf supply is repaired.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base

capacity_pipeline.change: Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap

War premium unwinds but structural deficit holds — consensus path

ai_analysis: Price regime: the war-premium spike to a 4-yr high ($3,855, 02-Jun) has unwound to ~$3,550 — a step DOWN in level, but the rebound off Monday's one-month low signals the structural-deficit floor is holding, not a fresh downtrend.

Term structure: the cash–3M backwardation has collapsed from 19-yr highs (~$69) to ~$4 — the clearest trend signal that acute front-end scarcity is easing, even as total LME stocks keep drawing (~320kt, −37% YTD).

Sentiment: the news flow skews two-way — bearish on the premium unwind and the oil drop, bullish on the intact physical deficit — netting roughly neutral-to-constructive; a decisive tilt likely around the 19-Jun signing.

Forward bias: consensus holds a firm 2026 ($3,300–3,600) easing into 2027 (~$3,000) as Gulf supply is repaired. Base case (50%) = a lower, choppier but supported price; the main upside risk is a slipped EGA/ALBA restart.

exec_summary: Aluminum corrected lower through the week, unwinding part of the early-June spike. LME cash settled at $3,536/t on Friday 12 June — down 5.4% from the prior-week close ($3,736/t, 5 June) and well off the four-year high of $3,855/t touched on 2 June — with a midweek trough of $3,489/t on 10 June. A firmer US dollar (DXY near 100) and a robust US jobs report, which restored Fed rate-hike leeway, pressured dollar-priced metals; even so, aluminum holds roughly +20% year-to-date and about +45% year-on-year.

The decisive catalyst was geopolitical de-escalation: on 14 June the US and Iran reached a framework agreement to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with President Trump ordering an end to the naval blockade and a memorandum of understanding slated for signature around 19 June. The prospect of restored Gulf flows collapsed the prompt scarcity premium — the cash-to-3M backwardation compressed from +$69/t (Monday) to +$4/t (Friday), near flat and off its 19-year highs. Physical tightness has not yet released, however: LME inventories drew a further ~11 kt to 319,925 t (fresh lows, ~1.4 days of global output), and the supply restart is slow — EGA's flagship plant needs ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA remains partially offline, and Gulf primary output fell to a decade-low ~330 kt in April (-35% YoY). Net read: structurally tight, but the war-risk premium has begun to deflate.

price_table.note: Off 4-yr high $3,855 (2 Jun); wk low $3,489 (10 Jun)
Curve flattening as war-risk premium unwinds
Fresh lows ~1.4 days output; draw continues despite price drop
Q2 oversupply weighing; Platts API region - verify PRA
Q2 ~$351.5/t - ~10-yr high; US Midwest also elevated (S.232)

drivers: US-Iran framework deal (announced 14 June) to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz - Trump ordered the naval blockade lifted, with an MOU due ~19 June and the conflict to formally end within ~60 days. This de-escalation is the dominant new catalyst and began unwinding the war-risk premium, collapsing the cash-3M backwardation toward flat.

Macro turned to a headwind: a firmer dollar (DXY ~100) and a strong US jobs report lifted Fed-hike odds, pressuring dollar-priced metals; Brent held elevated near $93/bbl on residual Gulf risk. US CPI/PPI are the next swing factor.

Physical market still tight despite the sell-off: LME stocks fell to fresh lows of ~320 kt (~1.4 days of global output, ~92% Russian-origin), and the Gulf restart is slow - EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline, Gulf primary output at a decade-low ~330 kt in April (-35% YoY).
China exports kept filling the gap: unwrought aluminum and products rose ~5.7% MoM to 632 kt in May (five-month cumulative 2.69 Mt, +10.4% YoY), even as domestic output runs near the 45 Mt cap.
Raw materials mixed: Guinea's tighter bauxite export controls add upstream cost pressure, while alumina FOB Australia stayed under Q2 oversupply pressure (softer indices) despite the Gulf disruption.

implications.text: the backwardation collapse and softer outright price reduce the urgency to sell prompt-only; the flattening curve opens a window to layer forward hedges on priced tonnes. Sustain value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA) and avoid over-committing forward fixed-price near the highs should de-escalation accelerate the unwind.

regional premiums remain supported (MJP Q2 settlement ~$351.5/t, a ~10-year high; US Midwest elevated on Section 232), but a Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk to premiums into 2H - prioritise near-term premium-share gains and lock favourable renewal terms before normalisation.

softer ex-China alumina (Q2 oversupply) trims net-seller upside; a reopened Strait would lower freight and war-risk insurance - begin unwinding contingency-routing premiums, re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure, and keep committed-cargo vessel cover without locking peak war-risk freight.

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition; track the ~19 June MOU signing and blockade-lifting mechanics, EGA/ALBA restart cadence, US CPI/PPI and the Fed, and China export/tariff dynamics as the tells for how fast premiums normalise.

outlook: US-Iran MOU signing (~19 June) and the timing/mechanics of lifting the naval blockade and reopening the Strait of Hormuz - the dominant supply and risk-premium wildcard.
US CPI and PPI prints and Fed commentary - the near-term driver of the dollar and dollar-priced metals after the firmer jobs report.
Whether LME stocks keep drawing and the cash-3M curve holds near flat or re-steepens - the real-time gauge of physical tightness versus de-escalation.
China export pace against the 45 Mt output cap, and any further US Section 232 tariff adjustments shaping regional premiums.

weekly.caveat: Note: SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
